answer:
Wildcard Model Resolution: LiteLLM does not require the incoming model to exactly match a concrete deployment name before it can participate in routing or fallback. In router.py, get_model_list() first tries direct deployment lookup, then alias lookup, and if that returns nothing it calls self.pattern_router.route(model_name). For each wildcard match, get_model_list materializes a deployment_typed_dict and rewrites the returned deployment's model_name to the requested model name, so a concrete request can continue through the router as the logical model group that was requested. In pattern_match_deployments.py, PatternMatchRouter uses _pattern_to_regex() to convert a wildcard like openai/* into a regex, PatternMatchRouter.route() uses re.match to find openai/gpt-4.1-mini, and _return_pattern_matched_deployments() deep-copies the deployment while rewriting litellm_params["model"] through set_deployment_model_name(). set_deployment_model_name() uses the matched dynamic segments, which is what turns an openai/* deployment pattern into the concrete openai/gpt-4.1-mini model for the call.

Initial Order Filtering: The first selection pass is restricted by order filtering. In utils.py, _get_deployment_order() reads order from litellm_params first and then model_info. _get_order_filtered_deployments() builds _valid_orders, computes min_order, and when target_order is not supplied it returns only deployments whose order equals min_order. In router.py, the deployment selection path pops _target_order from request_kwargs and calls _get_order_filtered_deployments(..., target_order=_target_order) before picking a deployment. On the initial request there is no _target_order, so the wildcard-backed candidate set is reduced to the minimum order group, for example order: 1, and order: 2 is not selected yet.

Order-Based Fallback Construction: When the first attempt fails, async_function_with_fallbacks_common_utils() builds same-model-group order retries before external fallback execution. It calls get_model_list(model_name=original_model_group, team_id=_request_team_id), which is important because that lookup is wildcard-aware for groups such as openai/* -> openai/gpt-4.1-mini. It then collects order_values, determines skip_up_to from the current _target_order or the first order value, and creates order_fallback_entries like {"model": original_model_group, "_target_order": o} for every higher order. It also resolves external_fallback_group from configured fallbacks, but combined_fallbacks is built with order_fallback_entries first and external_fallback_group after that. In other words, the code explicitly puts order fallbacks first, so order: 2 in the same model group is tried before an external fallback.

External Fallback Matching and Execution: External fallback matching is handled in fallback_event_handlers.py. get_fallback_model_group() checks exact fallback keys, then _check_stripped_model_group(), then generic * fallbacks. _check_stripped_model_group() removes the provider prefix, so a fallback key like gpt-4.1-mini can match a requested model group like openai/gpt-4.1-mini. The combined list is then passed to run_async_fallback() as fallback_model_group. In run_async_fallback(), the code loops for mg in fallback_model_group; string fallbacks set kwargs["model"], dict fallbacks use kwargs.update(mg), then it increments fallback_depth, stores fallback_depth and max_fallbacks in kwargs, and re-enters async_function_with_fallbacks(*args, **kwargs). For synthetic order fallbacks, that dict carries _target_order back into normal deployment selection. For an external fallback model with no order, _get_order_filtered_deployments() sees that target_order has no matching deployment and returns all healthy deployments; the code comment calls out the external fallback model case. That is why the external fallback still runs instead of being filtered away by a stale _target_order.

Final Conclusion: LiteLLM intentionally composes these mechanisms. Wildcard resolution through PatternMatchRouter and get_model_list lets openai/gpt-4.1-mini participate in an openai/* route. The first router pass uses _get_order_filtered_deployments to keep only the minimum order. On failure, async_function_with_fallbacks_common_utils synthesizes higher-order same-group retries and places those order fallbacks before external fallbacks. Only after those intra-group retries are exhausted does external fallback matching and run_async_fallback execution take over. The regression tests test_router_order_no_fallback_when_healthy, test_router_order_fallback_on_failure, test_router_order_fallback_then_external_fallback, and test_router_order_fallback_with_wildcard_model_group cover the same minimum-order, higher-order fallback, external fallback, and wildcard fallback behavior.

cot.content: Trace this as four connected mechanisms: wildcard resolution, first-pass order filtering, same-group order fallback construction, and external fallback matching/execution.

arguments.command: rg -n 'def get_model_list|pattern_router\.route|def _get_order_filtered_deployments|def _get_deployment_order|async_function_with_fallbacks_common_utils|def get_fallback_model_group|def run_async_fallback' litellm/router.py litellm/utils.py litellm/router_utils/fallback_event_handlers.py litellm/router_utils/pattern_match_deployments.py

litellm/utils.py:4895:def _get_deployment_order

litellm/router_utils/fallback_event_handlers.py:45:def get_fallback_model_group
litellm/router_utils/fallback_event_handlers.py:85:async def run_async_fallback
litellm/router_utils/pattern_match_deployments.py:52:class PatternMatchRouter

Regression tests cover initial minimum-order selection, fallback to higher order on failure, external fallback after order exhaustion, and wildcard model groups advancing across order levels.

groundings.inference: get_model_list resolves direct matches, aliases, and wildcard pattern matches, then rewrites wildcard matches so they participate under the requested model name.
PatternMatchRouter converts wildcard patterns to regexes, finds matching requests, and returns copied matched deployments.
set_deployment_model_name replaces wildcard segments in the deployment model with dynamic segments from the matched request.
_get_deployment_order reads order from litellm_params or model_info, and _get_order_filtered_deployments selects min_order or returns all deployments when target_order has no match.
The router pops _target_order from request_kwargs and passes it into _get_order_filtered_deployments before choosing a deployment.
async_function_with_fallbacks_common_utils uses wildcard-aware get_model_list, builds higher-order _target_order fallback entries, appends external fallbacks after them, and calls run_async_fallback.
get_fallback_model_group supports exact fallback keys, stripped-provider fallback keys, and generic fallback keys.
run_async_fallback iterates fallback_model_group, updates kwargs for each fallback, and recursively calls async_function_with_fallbacks.
Regression tests cover initial minimum-order selection, fallback to higher order on failure, external fallback after order exhaustion, and wildcard model groups advancing across order levels.
